videos.transcript: There's a lot more to Barcelona than just the beach and in today's video I'll be sharing an honest roundup of the best things to do in Barcelona, after many trips and tourist traps endured. In case you're new here, hi, I'm Christina from HappyToWander.com and this channel is all about practical travel advice from someone who's been there, done that, so be sure to like and subscribe if that sounds like your kind of thing and be sure to stick around until the end for a free map that has everything from this video pinned for your convenience. Now let's move on to the first Barcelona must-do; which is to drool over all the amazing modernist architecture. There are few cities in the world that look like Barcelona, with its mix of Gothic and modern architecture right by the beach, its unique grid layout in the Eixample neighborhood, plus its assortment of whimsical and unique buildings in the style of Catalan Modernism. So when in town, make sure you take the time to admire these stunning works, though be sure to secure your tickets well in advance if you want to see their interiors because they do sell out quickly. We'll start with the most famous example of this architectural style in Barcelona; the jaw-dropping Sagrada Familia, a creation by modernist icon Antoni Gaudí that has been in the works for over 140 years and is still not complete. With three intricate facades and 18 towers, there's truly no church like it in the world; with distinctive elements including a lack of right angles anywhere in the design and very few straight lines. Out of Gaudí's many impressive and expensive works in Barcelona this is the one I find most worth visiting on the inside,. Even though it is unfinished, I consider it one of the most spectacular churches in the world, with intricate details inspired by both religion and the natural world combining to create a maximalist and colorful church unlike any you've seen before. Another key attraction by Gaudí is Park Güell. This whimsical park perched on Carmel Hill offers not just wonderful views over the city but some of the most imaginative and colorful spaces in Barcelona. Walking into Park Güell is like stepping into the fanciful imagination of Gaudí himself, with colored glass, unique ceramic work and nature inspired elements all effortlessly woven with the park's natural surroundings. There's also Casa Vicens. This was kind of Gaudí's first big job after graduating and while for over 130 years this mansion was private property, it is now a museum that you can visit. While I admit it's not that spectacular on the inside, apart from a few rooms, it's still well worth a look if you're in search of something a little different, even just for the facade, which is one of my personal favorites in the city. And on the elegant Passeig de Gràcia, you'll find two more iconic Gaudí buildings: La Pedrera, whose unique stone design resembles an open quarry, and Casa Batlló with its colorful mosaic facade, mask-shaped balconies and scaly roof said to resemble the back of a dragon. Both of these houses are well worth seeing at least from the outside, though going inside is both expensive and unbelievably crowded, so I wouldn't necessarily recommend it unless you're a huge fan of architecture. Of course, while Gaudí is the most famous name linked to Catalan Modernism, he is far from the only notable architect whose work you can admire in Barcelona. The block occupied by Casa Batlló, for instance, is known as the Mansana de la Discòrdia or Block of Discord, thanks to the presence of three modernist facades by three different architects, all fighting for our attention. Gaudí's Casa Batlló is, of course, the most crowded and popular but right next door is the Casa Amatller by Josep Puig i Cadafalch, and down the block also Casa Lleó Morera by Lluís Domènech i Montaner. I did Casa Batlló and Casa Amatller back to back and let me tell you, the difference was astounding. I practically had a private tour at Casa Amatller, which was gorgeous on the inside and I can definitely recommend paying them a visit. Speaking of i Montaner, he is responsible for two of my other favorite modernist structures in Barcelona; firstly, the Palau de la Música Catalana, one of the most stunning concert halls I've ever seen with a richly decorated interior filled with stained glass and striking natural motifs all around; and the Hospital de la Santa Creu i Sant Pau, located a short walk away from the Sagrada Familia. This vast complex is actually considered the largest modernist complex in the world and operated as a functional hospital until 2009, before being converted into a museum and cultural center today. Filled with bold colors, vibrant mosaics and plenty of stained glass, this is one of the most beautiful places to visit in the city, with plenty of space to roam around making it feel much less crowded than its more famous counterparts. Now with architecture out of the way, let's move on to the next must-do in Barcelona, which is to dive into the city's epic food culture. With over 8,000 restaurants to choose from in the city, visitors are really spoiled for choice when they visit Barcelona, which of course, makes decisions ever more difficult. For a more guided experience, I can definitely recommend taking a food tour, something I did on my first Barcelona trip. Having local expertise really helped me get my bearings and cut out the guesswork of where to eat. You can also soak in the city's foodie culture by visiting one of its many fresh food markets. La Boqueria is no doubt the most popular, filled with all sorts of colorful produce stalls, as well as many bars to sit and grab food. Thanks to its popularity among tourists, however, prices here are known to be inflated but it's definitely worth taking a look. There are also more local-oriented markets worth checking out too, like the beautiful Santa Caterina Market with its vibrant wavy rooftop, as well as the Sant Antoni Market, which also has a book market on Sundays. And while Barcelona is a cosmopolitan city with options representing cuisines from around the world, when in town do try your best to indulge in Catalan cuisine as much as possible. While most tourist-oriented restaurants will try to sell you on paella and sangria, Catalonia has many more traditional dishes to offer, including the ubiquitous pa amb tomàquet; fideuà, which is like a local twist on paella except with short noodles instead of rice; and bombas, a fried potato croquet that's stuffed with a tasty meat filling then topped with sauce, an invention of the Barceloneta neighborhood. Most typical of all though, might be to partake in fer el vermut, a pre-meal ritual that usually involves having a drink with some bar snacks like olives and jamón, while mixing and mingling, kind of like a local take on the Italian aperitivo. There are many bars that offer this experience, so don't miss it. Now if you don't mind paying a little extra for a gorgeous venue to drink and enjoy a bite, I'd recommend visiting El Nacional, which is home to several restaurants and bars housed in an extravagant light-filled hall. We sat at one of the bars in the middle and enjoyed some tasty snacks while people watching. Overall, Barcelona is full of great eats, so be sure to do your research, read reviews and take advantage. Just be sure to steer clear of restaurants with lots of pictures and menus in various languages, especially if they're located in busy locations like Las Ramblas or near the beach as these are, more often than not, tourist traps that serve mediocre food. Next on our list is a wander around the Gothic Quarter. This postcard-perfect piece of Barcelona is where many say the city's history began, when Romans first established a settlement here thousands of years ago. Today, the neighborhood is known for its network of narrow winding streets that link a variety of squares and monuments, the most famous of which is the beautiful Barcelona Cathedral which costs a few euro for entry but is open for free during set worship and prayer times each day. The inside is well worth seeing with a dramatic Gothic interior as well as a nice rooftop that you can visit for amazing views of the city. Elsewhere in this neighborhood, there's also the lively Plaça Reial, filled with palm trees and art galleries, crammed with restaurants and bars. Granted, the prices here are a bit inflated but there's still quite a nice vibe. Another popular spot is Carrer del Bisbe, where you'll find the most photographed bridge in the city. And while the Gothic Quarter is notoriously crowded with tourists most of the time, there are still some cool hidden gems scattered around, including some Roman artifacts and landmarks, like the Temple of Augustus where you will see tall Roman columns dating back to the 1st century BC. Overall, this is a beautiful piece of Barcelona to explore but if you can, I'd recommend visiting early in the morning before it gets too crowded. While here you can continue your explorations with a visit to El Born and La Ribera. Located close to the beach and overflowing with cafes and restaurants, walking around these conjoined neighborhoods is another fun Barcelona activity I can highly recommend. Highlights include the Basilica of Santa Maria del Mar: a beautiful gothic church, outside of which you'll find the Fossar de les Moreres, a plaza with an eternal flame memorializing the Catalan soldiers fallen during the War of the Spanish Succession, as well as the El Born Cultural Center, where you can scope out some really cool archaeological ruins. There's also several museums here, including the Picasso Museum and Moco Museum, dedicated to Modern Art, in addition to some of the spots I actually mentioned before like the Santa Caterina Market, plus plenty of cool shops and boutiques. Now once you get tired of roaming narrow streets, another must-do in Barcelona is to relax in the Parc de la Ciutadella. This tranquil green oasis in the city is home to a few notable must-sees, including the stunning Cascada Monumental, designed with the help of a young Gaudí, as well as its ornate entryway: Barcelona's own Arc de Triomf. Inside the park, you'll find lots of spots for picnicking, relaxing or even renting a boat, so be sure to put this on your list for a more relaxed activity. Another relaxed activity to enjoy is to get an amazing view over the city. Barcelona is a truly epic city with no shortage of views. For the best free one though, I can recommend visiting the Bunkers del Carmel which offers a 360° view over Barcelona, including iconic parts of the skyline like the Sagrada Familia, as well as the beach. There's of course, also many paid views as well, including atop the famous Columbus Monument and the lookout from the Torre Glòries. Perhaps most fun of all though, is to simply relax and chill at a rooftop bar, of which Barcelona has too many to count! Better yet, book a hotel with a terrace and just relax. Speaking of amazing views, another Barcelona must-do is to explore Montjuïc. This hill is packed with attractions and makes for a great half-day outing during your stay in the city. Getting there through the famous Montjuïc cable car takes you on a 750 meter journey suspended above Barcelona and once you get to the top and work your way down there is so much you can do, like visit a 17th century castle and fortress, soak up amazing views, visit unique museums like the National Art Museum of Catalonia, the Fundació Joan Miró and the Poble Espanyol. Or explore a range of cool green spaces like the Botanical Gardens and the Anella Olímpica, the park specially built for the 1992 Olympics. Another highlight here are the Magic Montjuïc Fountains, constructed during the 1929 Barcelona International Exposition, these fountains are now one of the best-known attractions in the city, with a dazzling light, music and water show that wows millions of visitors each year. From here, you can also scope out the impressive Venetian Towers in Plaça d'Espanya, which are modeled after the Campanile of St Mark's Basilica in Venice. Now moving on to another hill offering plenty of fun in Barcelona, we have Tibidabo. At 512 meters this is Barcelona's tallest hill and is home to Spain's oldest amusement park with many of its original rides, for better or worse, still intact. A highlight here, besides the rides, is the Sacred Heart of Jesus Church of Atonement; a beautiful church with a lengthy name topped with a monumental statue of Jesus that you can see up close through an elevator ride. Now heading back to sea level now, another fun Barcelona activity is to hit the beach. For a lot of eager vacationers, time in Barcelona is synonymous with tanning time, with the most popular pick being Barceloneta Beach. As most locals will tell you, however, this is far from the best beach, with a lot of litter, crowds and guys selling watererd down Mojitos and beach towels. I wouldn't necessarily plan to spend a day here tanning and swimming but I do admit it's a nice spot for sunset. That said, I think a nicer activity might be to take a sunset boat cruise. I did this on my most recent trip and really enjoyed it. While you don't go far, you do get to enjoy a range of sights from mega-yachts along the Marina Port Vell, to Barceloneta Beach landmarks and wide open sea. All with bottomless cava and snacks along the way, if you so choose. Book a ride for sunset time and you definitely won't regret it. Now at this point, you might be thinking, what about Las Ramblas? While it's commonly listed in every guide as one of the best things to do in Barcelona, I don't necessarily agree. Known as the city's main pedestrian promenade, Las Ramblas is a 1.2 km stretch that extends from Port Vell to Plaça de Catalunya. Along the way you'll mostly encounter street performers, tourist trap restaurants and crowds, lots and lots of crowds. Honestly, I wouldn't recommend spending much time here on this strip since it's crowded and riddled with a lot of opportunistic criminals, like pickpockets and street scammers but there are some noteworthy sites in and around here; including La Boqueria Market, as mentioned before, and some unique pieces of architecture. I would maybe just walk down once to say you've done it but otherwise avoid it throughout your trip. I have similar sentiments about Plaça de Catalunya, which is Barcelona's main square and often touted as a Barcelona must-see. In my opinion, it's just okay and while you'll probably pass through it thanks to its central location, it's definitely more of a pigeon and transport hub than somewhere you'd actually kick back and relax, so don't prioritize it too much. Finally, don't forget about the many, many amazing day trips that you can enjoy from Barcelona. There's options for every preference whether you want mountains, seaside or more cities. For mountain lovers, a visit to dreamy Montserrat is a must: with striking mountains, verdant greenery and an epic monastery wedged between its peaks. Meanwhile, beach lovers should definitely enjoy some gems along the idyllic Costa Brava, with beautiful beach towns like Tossa de Mar located just a short drive north of Barcelona. Finally, if you're into cities I can highly recommend Girona, home to stony medieval architecture, picturesque riverbanks and plenty of nerdy photo ops for Game of Thrones fans, given the city's stint as Braavos. I can also recommend Tarragona, which is home to cool Roman ruins, glossy gold beaches and super fresh seafood, all with a fraction of Barcelona's crowds. Overall, there's no shortage of things to do in and around Barcelona, so be sure to consult my free map accessible here for all these places we discussed, along with food and drink recommendations. Thanks for watching, I'll see you next week, bye!

videos.transcript: In this video, we'll show you the top 10 things to do in Barcelona. The suggestions are based on our fun trip to this beautiful city. Don't forget to like this video, subscribe to our channel, and enable notifications. And share your own experience in the comments below. We want to thank Ceptics, the number one choice for travel adapters on Amazon, for sponsoring this video. The link is in the description. And stick around until the end of this video because we have a bonus for you. Here are our top 10 picks: Number 10: Park Güell Park Güell is a public park designed by the Spanish architect Antoni Gaudí. The park was built between the year 1900 and 1914 and it officially opened as a public park in 1926. It is located on Carmel hill in the Gracia district and features captivating architectural elements, sculptures, and landscaping. The park is known for its vibrant mosaics, gingerbread houses, and imaginative use of color and form. Park Güell was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1984. Don’t forget to visit the most famous Area with the lucky lizard or sit on the long mosaic bench shaped like a sea serpent. Don't forget to visit other works of art by Antoni Gaudi, like Casa Milà or Casa Battló. Number 9: La Sagrada Família Talking about Antoni Gaudi, let’s continue to his most famous masterpiece - La Sagrada Familia, a large Roman Catholic church, considered one of the greatest works of architecture in the world. Construction of the church began in 1882 and still continues to this day, with an estimated completion in 2026. The church is renowned for its striking, organic architecture and richly detailed façades, adorned with sculptures and intricate carvings. It is one of Spain's most visited tourist attractions and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Francisco De Paula del Villar was initially the chief architect of the Sagrada Família. However, he resigned after a year and was replaced by Gaudi, who incorporated Gothic and Art Nouveau styles into the church's design. Did you know that Antoni Gaudi is buried in the underground level of La Sagrada Famiglia? And don’t forget to visit the stunning interior of the cathedral or landscaped gardens of Plaça de Gaudí. Number 8: La Rambla and La Boqueria Market La Rambla is a popular and a busy street lined with shops, restaurants, cafes, and street performers. It stretches about 1.2 kilometers or 0.7 miles from Plaça de Catalunya to the Christopher Columbus Monument at the Port Vell Marina. La Rambla was originally a sewage-filled stream serving as an important drain for the heavy rainwater that flowed from the hills in the spring and fall. Now, thousands of tourists wander around this unique street every day, making it one of the city's major attractions. The Legend says that drinking from the Canaletes Fountain will make you fall in love with Barcelona and return to the city time and again. La Rambla is also notorious for its scammers and thieves, so much so, that Barcelona has been nicknamed the pickpocket capital of the world. Always watch your belongings and use common sense. Located on La Rambla is also La Boqueria - an extensive public market dating back to the 13th century. The market is known for its wide variety of fresh produce, meats, seafood, and other food products. There are also several bars and restaurants inside the market where you can enjoy traditional Spanish food, like paella, tapas, and many others. Number 7: Parc de la Ciutadella Established in the mid-19th century, Parc de la Ciutadella is considered the greenest oasis in Barcelona. Conveniently located close to the city center, this historical garden covers an area of about 170 acres. It features gardens, lakes, playgrounds, a zoo, museums, and monuments. The park is also home to several important landmarks, including the Arc de Triomf, a triumphal arch built to commemorate the 1888 Barcelona Universal Exposition, and the Cascada. This large fountain is the park's most prominent feature. There are other beautiful parks in Barcelona. Check our travel guide for more suggestions. By the way, our mobile-friendly travel guide covers the top 20 things to do in Barcelona and things to know before you visit, including maps, opening hours, links to buy tickets, itinerary suggestions, and other information. Number 6: Barceloneta Barceloneta is a lively neighborhood known for its sandy beach, which can get extremely crowded during the summer, seafood restaurants, and vibrant nightlife. You can enjoy food and drinks while admiring the sea view at the numerous beach bars. Barceloneta was originally a fishermen's quarter built in the 18th century but is now also home to famous beach clubs. The Legend says that this place inspired Miguel de Cervantes for the famous fight scene between Don Quixote and the Knight of the White Moon. The Area is also home to the five-star W Hotel, famous for its sail-like shape. Don't miss the nearby Rambla de Mar, a waterfront harbor that is a part of the Port of Barcelona or ride a port cable car with beautiful views of the city and surroundings. Number 5: Tibidabo Mount Tibidabo is the tallest point in Barcelona, with stunning views of the city and the surrounding area. The hill is home to several popular tourist attractions, including the Tibidabo Amusement Park, one of the oldest amusement parks in Europe, opened in 1899; The Tibidabo Cathedral considered one of the most important religious monuments in Barcelona and the Torre de Collserola telecommunications tower. Tibidabo is an excellent destination for those looking to enjoy stunning city views and some fun at the amusement park. Don't skip the nearby Observatori Fabra. Number 4: Camp Nou Camp Nou is a must-see destination for FC Barcelona football fans and those interested in the history of one of the most successful football clubs in the world. The stadium can seat over 99,000 spectators, making it the largest stadium in Spain and Europe and the third-largest in the world in terms of capacity. It offers visitors a chance to learn about the history of one of the most successful football clubs in the world and to see a vast collection of trophies, shirts, and memorabilia. Camp Nou is a frequent venue for high-profile concerts like that of Julio Iglesias and U2. If you are a sports fan, don't miss Anella Olímpica on Montjuic hill, a sports complex and the main site for the 1992 Summer Olympics. This video is sponsored by Ceptics. If you’re traveling to Barcelona and you're coming from outside Europe, you will probably need a special European travel adaptor to charge your phone and other devices. Did you know different adapters charge your phone, laptop, and other devices at different speeds? Sometimes charging goes nowhere, and, when traveling, you need your devices ready to go fast. Use powerful travel adapters with USB, USB-C and other plugs from Ceptis for a quick charge; some even offer the ability to charge 7 devices simultaneously. You can even buy a voltage converter for small electronics like a toothbrush, shaver, and straightener that you otherwise couldn’t use. Visit Ceptics website to find adapters that fit your needs. The link is in the description. Number 3: Montjuïc Castle Montjuïc Castle is a fortress located on Montjuïc hill with a long and interesting history, dating back to the 17th century when it was built by the Spanish military to protect the city from foreign invasions. During the Spanish Civil War, it was used as a prison and execution site. Today, the castle is open to the public as a museum, where visitors can learn about the castle's history and enjoy views of the city from the hilltop. Hop on the Montjuic cable car for a captivating 750 m-long trip to the castle. (Check our travel guide for more suggestions.) Number 2: The Magic Fountain The Magic Fountain of Montjuïc, located close to the beautiful Plaça d'Espanya, just below Palau Nacional, is a large fountain built in 1929 for the International Exhibition held in the city that year. It has been a popular attraction in Barcelona ever since, featuring a choreographed display of water, light, and music, creating a mesmerizing show. The fountain was severely damaged in the Spanish Civil War and could not function until 1955. In the 1980s, music was incorporated into the light show. The fountain was fully restored before the Summer Olympics events in the nineties. The fountain is open to the public, and the shows are free of charge, typically taking place every 30 min in the evening. Number 1: Gothic Quarter The Gothic Quarter, also known as the Barri Gòtic, is a historic neighborhood characterized by its narrow corridors and a labyrinth of small streets opening out into squares, its many Gothic-style buildings, and structures, including the famous Cathedral of Barcelona, which dates back to the 14th century. The Gothic Quarter is one of the oldest parts of the city, also featuring the remains of the Roman wall. Today, the neighborhood is a popular destination for tourists and locals, with an array of shops, restaurants, bars, and important historical and cultural sites. The quarter is also home to various medieval landmarks dating – not back to the Middle Ages as one might expect from the name – but to the late 19th and early 20th centuries, like Pont del Bisbe. And here is the bonus that we promised. If you have an extra day, explore the Montserrat mountain range, with a beautiful monastery located about 40 miles or 60 km northwest of Barcelona. It is a popular tourist destination and pilgrimage site known for its stunning views, unique rock formations, and the Benedictine Monastery of Montserrat. You can take the scenic cable car ride or even the funicular to get to the top. We wish you an amazing trip to Barcelona.

videos.transcript: Hey there fellow travelers, Mark here with Wolters' World, and today we're in Cordoba, Spain, and today we're going to talk about our the don'ts of coming to Spain so you can definitely have a better time when you do come here. And I'm going to start with something that seems weird that I'm going to start at the beginning, but I think it's really important for you to know, and that is don't say adios when you leave when you're in Spain. You want to say hasta luego cuz here hasta luego means see you until later, see you later, because you never know when hasta luego it may be. It might be, oh, when I see you at the hotel breakfast the next day, or when I see you in the town sometime, or when you come back in 10 years' time and bring your family to enjoy Spain. Cuz hasta luego is the way you say it, and honestly, don't forget to say it when you're coming out of an elevator cuz there's like greetings are really important here. So, hasta luego is the way to go, not adios, okay? Another don't I have for you is when you're coming to Spain, a lot of people think it's just, you know, sun and heat all year round. Well, I got to tell you that, don't think Spain is like beaches and sun and heat and just, uh, so hot all year round. It's not. You come to Madrid in the wintertime, you need a winter coat. Like it snows there. Like go into a villa and you're like, why am I freezing my behind off? Because it's not always hot and sunny and beautiful when you're here. I mean, okay, it's always beautiful when you come to Spain. It's gorgeous, but the weather-wise, that's that's thing I think people mess up is they don't realize it's coming to Spain at different times of year, you're going to have very different weather that you need to be prepared for, okay? So, don't think it's just sunny beach warm weather all year round. I mean, don't get me wrong, you come to Sevilla or here in Cordoba in the summer and it's like 100° 30 38° C, you're like, I'm dying. But yeah, it's not always like that. Now, another don't I have for you, and this might be the most important safety don't I have when you come to Spain is, don't insult Spanish food or wine. Because the Spaniards, they're totally chill. Wondering what not to do in Barcelona? This Barcelona travel tips video summarizes all the important must-knows before visiting Barcelona for the first time, from how to get the best deals on tickets and transport to how to avoid tourist traps and make the most of a short time in Barcelona!
videos.transcript: If you're visiting Barcelona for the first time this year, make sure you watch this video because in it, I'm going to be sharing all the top tips and must-knows for first-time visitors. Now in case you're new here, hi, I'm Christina from HappyToWander.com and this channel is all about practical travel tips from someone who's been there, done that, so be sure to like and subscribe if that sounds like your kind of thing. Now, let's get started with some Barcelona travel planning tips. So of course, as you probably are aware, Barcelona is a city that is geographically located in Spain but many visitors forget that it is part of a state known as Catalonia, which is culturally unique from other parts of Spain and therefore culturally different from a lot of the Spanish stereotypes and clichés that you might have in your head. In fact, most Catalans will not refer to themselves as Spanish and there is a pretty heavy independence movement as well, but that's a story for another time. As a tourist, what you should know is that Barcelona is in Catalonia, so be prepared for signs in Catalan and be ready for some cultural traditions that are different from what many first-timers consider to be Spanish traditions. Next, definitely do avoid visiting Barcelona during peak season. The truth is, Barcelona is one of the most visited cities in Europe and even though I've never been in 'peak' peak season, I have seen enough crowds during my January, April, and May visits to be very afraid of just how many people there will be in the peak months of July and August. Add on the sweltering heat and it's probably not the best recipe for a good time. So if you don't like crowds and you have the flexibility, definitely timing your visit for the off-season is something I would highly recommend, especially during my visit in January, there were so many monuments I could just walk up and go see and really, the temperatures were so comfortable and so long as you're not there specifically to go laze on the beach, going in the off-season is really, really awesome if you're there for the cultural and historic attractions. However, I do need to clarify; just because Barcelona is quieter in the off-season doesn't mean it's a magical time where there's going to be no one there at all, in fact, there are still crowds during the off-season - even in January - so do be prepared for that. Like I said, this is one of the most popular cities to visit in Europe, so be sure to have your expectations in check. In regards to where to stay, there are so many different options in Barcelona depending on your budget. Overall for first-time visitors, I would highly recommend you stay in the central historic core, meaning neighborhoods like the Gothic Quarter, El Borne, Eixample or Gracia, which will ensure you're within walking distance to most points of interest and also will put you really close to a lot of restaurants and nightlife. I have stayed in several places before that I can highly recommend, so here's some quick recommendations: on a more budget-friendly price point if you're looking for a hostel, in the past, I've stayed at the Sant Jordi Sagrada Familia Hostel and everything was really clean, really great location and I can highly recommend that if you're looking for a hostel. If you're looking for a hotel, budget-wise, I stayed at the Hotel Praktik Garden and that's actually a chain that they have in Barcelona, so a few different locations, really good value for money. In terms of mid-range options, I've stayed at the Gran Via and it's a super, super central location and has really beautiful communal areas; and on the bougier, more elevated aesthetic side of things, I can definitely recommend staying at Seventy Hotel, and I really like staying at Casa Bonay as well. If you're feeling especially splurge-y, there are quite a few well-known hotels that have really amazing views if you're looking for a kind of bucket list experience but that said, do be very careful because a lot of the time, these hotels will, of course, charge extra for any rooms that have this iconic view. So always double-check when you're booking and don't just assume you're going to get a Sagrada Familia view just because you booked at that hotel. Some popular ones include Hotel Colon, which has epic views of the cathedral; Ayre Hotel, which has a bar overlooking the Sagrada Familia; and also the Hoxton Pobleneu, which has a nice terrace, views and pool. And I'll be linking all these recommendations in the description, by the way, in case you want to find links. Now before we move on to transport tips, I just want to say: when you're making your itinerary, something I highly recommend is adding a few more Catalan destinations on your itinerary because Barcelona is awesome, but actually, the entire state of Catalonia has so much to offer, so some other cities I really love include Girona and Tarragona; Montserrat is absolutely incredible if you love mountains; and if you can go a little farther, the Costa Brava, that coastline is absolutely stunning and there's lots of beautiful towns around there as well. Now let's move on to some Barcelona tips for transport and getting around. In terms of arriving in the city, the cheapest way is definitely taking the Aerobus from the airport, which costs 5.90 each way and is super quick and convenient. If you have a larger group or if you're just looking for ease of mind though, booking a private transfer can be quite cost-effective as well. For the simplest experience, I like using Welcome Pickups: they provide you with an English-speaking driver that tracks your flight and everything; but the cheapest option is probably getting an Uber, which is actually really handy for getting around the city as well. Now once you're in the city, honestly, if you're staying in a central neighborhood, the best way to get around is just on foot. The architecture in Barcelona is probably one of the best highlights for any visitor, so being able to walk around really lets you absorb everything and see everything and experience it properly. That said, if you're staying a little farther away or if you need to traverse some longer distances, then the public transport is actually very convenient and affordable in Barcelona as well. Buses and metro trains both use the same ticket, so it's very simple with the ticketing process, you can buy them just at the machines. But I will say if you think you will be using public transport quite frequently, one really good ticket to buy is the T10 ticket, which is essentially a packet of 10 different tickets and the cost works out to be way cheaper than buying single tickets each time, and it also saves you the stress of having to buy a single ticket every time you want to take the metro. They also do have 48-hour and 72-hour passes but to be honest with you, they're quite expensive and if you're just staying in the center of the city, you're going to be doing a lot of walking, so for most visitors, unless you're staying farther away, I don't really think it makes sense to get one of these longer passes. As I said before, Ubers and taxis are also a very good way to bridge longer distances and it's quite affordable as well, Uber works perfectly fine in Barcelona. So definitely keep that in mind, because this can actually work out to be way cheaper if you have a bigger group. You can also rent a bike or scooter as needed, there's tons of options available. Now let's move on to some Barcelona tips for attractions. The major tip I have for you is to book big attractions well in advance. If there's anything in Barcelona that you've always dreamt of visiting, make sure you secure those tickets well in advance, I'm talking weeks, even months, just because again, Barcelona is so busy and most of the tourists who are coming here are going to see the exact same things as you want to see, so you want to avoid disappointment by booking things well in advance. When in Barcelona, time is money, you don't want to be left waiting in line either, so if there's ever a skip the line option definitely get that as well and I'll be linking lots of different options for you in the description too. Now if you plan to do a lot of sightseeing though, something that might make sense is taking advantage of combo cards and different attraction passes. This can often help you to save money because honestly, sightseeing in Barcelona can be wildly expensive. With a lot of the Gaudi attractions, those can be 20 to 30 euro each which really adds up if you want to see many of them, so a lot of the time, if you're planning on going to a lot of attractions, it does make sense to get a pass of some kind and that's usually going to save you some money. The trouble is, there's so many of these passes so it can make it really overwhelming to decide. So when you're comparing the different passes, some of the distinctions to keep in mind include firstly, do they include public transport or not? Again, whether you need this is going to be up to you, for me, Barcelona is very walkable so a lot of the time you don't need that public transport pass but do keep in mind, some passes include that, some passes don't. Second, is looking at what kind of attractions the pass actually includes because each of these passes tend to cater towards a different type of attraction or they have a different selection, that's kind of what sets them apart so if you're there and you're really interested in the modernist attractions, for instance, you want to see a lot of Gaudi, not all the passes include the Gaudi attractions, so definitely keep that in mind and make sure you're reading the fine print to see what's actually included in the pass. On my most recent visit, I got a Go City Pass and that actually included most of the things that I wanted to see, most of the Gaudi attractions were on there and a lot of really enticing offers like day trips as well that were included, so it ended up saving me so much money. So I can definitely recommend that but at the end of the day, the best thing to do is really just crunch the numbers and do the math and then see which option works out to be the cheapest for what you specifically want to see. So some of the other passes that you might be interested in include the Barcelona Card, which is the official tourist card by the city of Barcelona, that includes public transport, several free museums, and also discounts on bigger attractions. There's the Art Ticket, which includes entry to six of Barcelona's top art museums for only 38 euro. The Barcelona Pass Modernista, which offers 7-day skip-the-line access to 10 of the most famous modernist works in Barcelona for steep savings, plus quite a few more. So again, compare the options, do the math: I have a free spreadsheet that I'll link in the description that can help with that but yeah, do keep in mind that these attraction passes can save you a ton of money. Now speaking of all these attractions though, something I will caution against is over stuffing your itinerary. This is very easy to do in Barcelona because, of course, there's so many amazing things to see but really, a lot of what makes Barcelona special is the vibe, so don't force yourself to see 10 museums in a day just to cram it all in, because you do need to leave some breathing space for enjoying some drinks on a terrace and enjoying some good food and just soaking in the vibes that make Barcelona special. Another tip is to wake up early. The morning hours are definitely the best time to visit Barcelona because after 10:00 a.m., most of the main tourist attractions are super, super busy. So yes, I am talking sunrise early if you want to be able to experience the city as if it's completely your own. So I can definitely recommend that as an option and what I would personally do, is pick maybe the number one attraction you're super keen to see and aim to get there first thing in the morning, and then plan a sunrise walk before that and then you can enjoy the city, enjoy the beauty and then have a crowd-free experience at your preferred place. I would also advise you kind of mix things up and go do some more offbeat activities that aren't just like the Gaudi top 10. Barcelona can be so busy at the main tourist attractions, sometimes uncomfortably so but honestly, there are so many cool places that will have a fraction of the crowds. So one of my favorite places, for instance, is the Recinte Modernista de Sant Pau Hospital, which is way less busy than any Gaudi spots. I also recently went to Casa Amatller, which is literally right next door to the Casa Batlló, but going there I genuinely had a private tour all to myself, which is crazy because the Casa Batlló, it was so crowded I was basically just walking in a line the entire time. So definitely don't get hung up on just the super famous places you've heard of, be sure to do some research and check out some more hidden gems as well. And I do have a full video about the best things to do in Barcelona, so be sure to check that out if you want some inspiration. And I know these are some very specific tips but if you're still with me and you like this level of detail, then be sure to let me know by commenting 'Sangria' in the comments. Now for those on a budget, another tip is to just enjoy all of Barcelona's amazing free viewpoints. Barcelona is a stunning city with lots of great vantage points, many of which are free, so here are some of my favorites: the Bunkers Del Carmel, to me, are the best view in the city. A bit of work to reach but absolutely magical views. There's also Las Arenas de Barcelona, which is a bull-ring turned shopping mall that is now home to a free 360° viewing platform that overlooks Plaça d'Espanya. Access is free through the escalator or to take the elevator, it's like a euro. El Corte Inglés is also nice, it's a very popular shopping center and on the top floor, they have a really great view from their rooftop cafe; and Tibidabo is, of course, the highest viewpoint in Barcelona and has some incredible sweeping views of the entire city. Now if you're visiting Barcelona for some beach time, then something I will caution against is going just to Barceloneta Beach, because a lot of people consider this to be the top beach in Barcelona but it is, of course, the most crowded and honestly, not the cleanest or the nicest. A lot of locals will, in fact, avoid it like the plague and there are actually much better options that aren't too far away. Bogatell, for instance, is way cleaner and close by and I've also heard great things about Sitges, if you don't mind going a little bit outside of the city. Now in terms of attractions, I think I touched on this before but again, do keep in mind that Barcelona has Catalan traditions so don't go scouring the streets in search of things like flamenco and bullfights, those are not things that they do here, and you know, they do have flamenco performances and things that are very much catered to tourists, if you want that experience. I've done it and it's fine, but it's not really an accurate representation of what the regional culture is actually like, so just keep that in mind. And be sure to make time to visit some of Barcelona's other neighborhoods. After all, Barcelona is more than just a tourist hot spot, they have over 1.6 million residents that live there and there are lots of cool, vibrant neighborhoods to check out beyond just the historic center. One of my favorites is El Born, it's not really that far out of the way, in fact, it's very close to the main neighborhoods but still a lot of people seem to miss it for some reason and there's just a lot of really cool stuff there the streets are beautiful there's the El Born Cultural Center, which is very cool and they have some of the best croissants in the world at Pastisseria Hofmann, so be sure to go there and get the mascarpone croissant, your life will be changed. And on that note, it's time for some food and drink tips for Barcelona. Now first, definitely do try your best to avoid tourist trap restaurants, especially on Las Ramblas, and the reason I say that is because again, there's a lot of tourists in Barcelona and so many restaurants have started catering to the tourist crowd and don't really focus too much on quality or value for money just because they know people are just looking for a place to eat once, they don't need to work on customer retention or people coming back, so it is very commonly known that many of these tourist trap restaurants, especially on Las Ramblas, will just serve you heated up frozen paella or watered down sangria and usually for quite an extortionate cost. So be sure to do some research into where you're going to eat and don't just let yourself get so hungry that you sit down at the first place that you see. Some of the common red flags to look out for include pictures or stock photos of popular Spanish foods, so if they're advertising things like paella - which actually isn't local to the region by the way - or things that they know tourists are looking for, like free Wi-Fi, it's probably not a great sign. Another not so great sign is if they've got menus in many different languages and they're clearly targeting tourists. Again, this isn't necessarily going to mean the food will be terrible but it's not going to give you a great chance, so always look at the reviews in advance. Another thing is to expect to eat dinner later. So if you're accustomed to eating dinner before 9:00 p.m., honestly, the meal times in Barcelona will probably shock you but people just dine later in Barcelona, so do adjust accordingly and know that a lot of restaurants won't be open that early. Another tip is to check out lots of local markets. A lot of first-time visitors will already have La Boqueria on their itinerary but that one, of course, is super busy and it's not the only one that's worth checking out. So one of my favorites is Santa Catarina Market, it has this really beautiful wavy roof design and it's got more of a local vibe, so I can definitely recommend that and there are other markets to explore as well, so definitely take advantage of the vibrant market scene and not just La Boqueria. And be sure to consider a food tour as well. Barcelona has such a vibrant food scene and having a guide bring you to the best places to give you a sample of Catalan cuisine can be really, really helpful. I did a tour with Devour Barcelona and learned so much about what regional cuisine actually consisted of and it was a really great way to experience a lot of different dishes in one go, and I got to see lots of the city that I feel like I probably would have missed otherwise. And lastly, if you're at Barceloneta Beach, don't drink the weird mojitos that they're selling on the beach. Honestly, I've heard nothing but bad things about these beachside mojitos and they honestly look pretty gross, and same goes for a lot of the things that the guys on the beach are selling, like the different beach blankets; I have straight up seen a guy try to sell someone a beach blanket, when they didn't want it he folded it up and stuffed it down his pants to avoid the police, so I wouldn't be trusting any of the things that are being sold on the beaches, especially in the more touristy zones of the beach. Now finally, let's move on to some Barcelona tips for once you arrive. I would definitely say to learn some Spanish or some Catalan in advance. Even though in touristy settings a lot of people will speak English, I did find myself needing to rely on my broken Spanish quite a lot more than in other major European cities, so it'd definitely be handy to learn a few basic words just to be polite, and also to have Google Translate handy just in case. Another thing you should avoid is over tipping. Tipping isn't expected in Barcelona to the same extent as it is in North America, so usually rounding up or tipping a few extra euro is, of course, going to be welcomed but you don't need to tip 15 to 20% like you might in North America. Another thing is, definitely wear comfortable shoes. You're going to be doing so much walking in Barcelona, there's a lot of cobblestones, so you don't want to be caught out wearing uncomfortable shoes, that's definitely super important. And very important, do learn all the common pickpocket tricks that you might encounter in Barcelona because unfortunately, the city does have a really nasty reputation for having some of the craftiest pickpockets in Europe, so do always keep your belongings very close to you, do never leave your phone kind of just out in the open or in any place where it's just very easily snatchable, and do be very careful when you're out in public because unfortunately, pickpockets in Barcelona can be very crafty. And a final niche tip for you, do be sure to carry hand sanitizer and some tissue or some toilet paper with you because in a lot of public bathrooms, there doesn't seem to be soap or toilet paper, so don't assume that that's just going to be provided, do definitely bring your own just in case, even in nicer restaurants and bars! Alright, thanks so much for watching, be sure to like and subscribe for more travel videos just like this one and I'll see you next time - bye!

Now once you're in the city, honestly, if you're staying in a central neighborhood, the best way to get around is just on foot
The architecture in Barcelona is probably one of the best highlights for any visitor, so being able to walk around really lets you absorb everything and see everything and experience it properly
There's the Art Ticket, which includes entry to six of Barcelona's top art museums for only 38 euro

videos.transcript: We just spent six weeks in Barcelona and we saw travelers make the same mistakes over and over again. Eating at tourist strap restaurants, endlessly queuing at the Gaudi highlights. We even saw someone get pickpocketed right next to us. So, in this video, we're going to go over 24 of the biggest mistakes travelers make in Barcelona so you can avoid them. Don't get stuck outside Sagrada Familia. Imagine flying all the way to Barcelona to see the Sagrada Familia, but then when you get here, they don't let you inside. This happens every day because people don't realize there is no ticket booth here. You can only buy tickets online. And if I try to buy one right now, it's sold out for the next 4 days. However, if you just book 3 weeks ahead, you'll be totally good to go. Do not visit Park Guell at 11:00 a.m. Park Guell is one of Gaudi's masterpieces. this incredible park with fairy tale architecture. But a lot of people actually end up really disappointed here because they come at the wrong time. If you come here around noon, the viewpoints are going to be packed. Selfie sticks everywhere. And there's not a lot of shade in this park. So in summer, you're going to feel like a rotisserie chicken. So just do what we did last time. Come here at 9:00 a.m., right when it opens, and you're going to have the most magical morning. Do not eat on La Rambla. You did not fly all the way out to Barcelona to have microwaved paella. But a lot of these restaurants, that's exactly what they're going to serve you. This is tourist trap central. Most of the restaurants on this street have the same red flags. They've got a menu in five languages, photos of every dish that they serve, and a waiter who's trying to wave you in because they really desperately need some customers. A local would never ever eat here. Instead, we're going to get off the street and walk 5 minutes to get to Bar Canete. This is the best tapas restaurant that we have been to in Barcelona. It's so good. I honestly don't want to share it. But you do want to make reservations in advance. Or you can walk to Cal Pep, a restaurant in the El Born district. Not only do they have amazing seafood tapas, but you can also sit at the bar and watch them cook right in front of you. And the staff are super kind and helpful here. So, the next category of tips we're going to cover is money and safety. After that, we're also going to talk about pre-trip planning mistakes, things you should never do at a Barcelona restaurant, and of course, big cultural don'ts, because there's a lot of things that tourists accidentally do that actually are offensive to the locals here. Next up, don't underestimate the pickpockets. Now, Barcelona is very safe in general, but for pickpocketing is one of the worst cities in Europe. Last week, we were sitting in this Starbucks right here, and someone's backpack was stolen right off the back of their chair. Can you imagine just losing your passport and wallet in a split second? Pickpockets usually operate in busy areas like La Rambla, the Gothic Quarter, or even the metro. But don't worry, there's a few simple things you can do to avoid becoming an easy target. Never keep your phone or wallet in your back pockets. Only use your front pockets. When you're in a crowd, zip your bag. Keep it in front of you where you can see it. At a restaurant, try not to put your bag on the back of your chair. Just put it in your lap or loop strap around your leg. And don't leave your phone on the table outside so it can't get snatched. If you do these things, you don't need to worry. You're going to be fine. Do not use the Euronet ATMs. These machines are everywhere in Barcelona. Quite frankly, they are evil. They charge a €6 transaction fee and on top of that, another 13% fees because they have the worst exchange rate in the world. If you want to draw €100 here, you lose €19 in fees. Stand back... Just use real Spanish banks instead. Don't get a hundred euro fine. There are three rules that tourists accidentally break all the time in Barcelona and they can really cost you. First, when you take public transport, buying your ticket is just step one. You then also need to scan it here because otherwise you can just keep reusing it. If you forget about this and get a ticket inspection, that will cost you €100 per person. Second, last week, police issued over 1,000 fines for public drinking. So, it's totally fine to have a beer on a terrace, but don't do it while you're on the beach or just walking around, because that's actually illegal. And third, and this is a mistake that we almost made ourselves. Look. Woo! Woo! We rented one of these electrical scooters that you can find all around Barcelona. It's by an app called Yego. Super fun way to get around the city. But just know that you can only park these in designated areas. Otherwise, you can get a really big fine. So, if you do this, which I recommend, check the app to know where you can park it. Don't mess up tipping. When the bill comes here in Barcelona, don't feel pressured to leave a big tip. Waiters earn a full salary here, so a tip is just a small thank you. We just had some food and some drinks here on the Plaza Real, which is a beautiful square in the Gothic Quarter. The bill comes out to 36,20 euros. So, I might round up to 38 or 39 if the service was good. Very important though, if you can try the tip in cash because if you do it on the card, you're not sure if your waiter is ever going to actually see the money. So, now there's a lot more you need to know about restaurants like how to order and some things that travelers do that the locals actually find weird. But first, the mistakes you make before you even get on the plane. Don't buy the wrong ticket for Casa Batllo. This is the biggest mistake we made on this trip to Barcelona. Casa Batllo is one of Gaudi's most spectacular buildings, but crowds really kill the magic here. We visited at 9:00 a.m., which is right when it opens, but it was already packed with tour groups. What we didn't know is that there's also an 8:30 a.m. ticket, the first entry ticket that lets you go in half an hour before the door is officially open to everybody else. So, you can actually see it in peace. You can get that ticket on GetYourGuide, which is where we buy all our tours and tickets. GetYourGuide has over 150,000 activities in cities all over the world. We love them because you don't have to sign up to a different website for every attraction for your trip. You can just book everything in one place. We'll put the links to all the tickets that we recommend getting in the description box below so you know exactly which ones to get. But do make sure to buy them two to three weeks in advance if you can, especially in high season because they really do sell out. Don't assume July and August are the best time to visit. July and August are European school holidays, which means peak crowds and peak prices. And in August specifically, a lot of small family-run restaurants and neighborhood shops actually close down for their own vacation. And those are the exact places that give Barcelona such a nice vibe. So, it's not a bad time to visit, but if you have flexibility, May and June or September have almost identical weather and a lot less hassle. Now, this next one can literally mess up half your day here in Barcelona if you don't know about it. Don't expect things to be open all day. Here in Spain, a lot of small shops and restaurants close in the afternoon from about 2 to 5:00 p.m. People take a long lunch break and in the evening the city comes alive again. So, be sure to plan around this. Also, keep in mind that Monday or Sundays are often closing days, too. Don't stay in the wrong neighborhood. There's two neighborhoods you want to avoid staying in. The first is the Barcelonetta area. That's right next to the beach, but because of night life, it gets really noisy at night, and it's pretty far from all of the main sites. The other one is Raval. That's literally one of the only places that people often say feels sketchy after dark in Barcelona. So, where should you actually stay? One great option is the Eixample area. It's beautiful and you'll be right in the middle of the action cuz it's close to Casa Batllo, the Sagrada Familia. But our personal favorite is the El Born district because it has these beautiful narrow alleys that Barcelona is so famous for. It is low on crowds and high on really good restaurants. We stayed at the Park Hotel. They have a really beautiful rooftop pool. We really love the hotel, so if you want to check it out, I'll put the link in the description box below. Don't count on Uber. This part of Spain has never really liked Uber because they want to protect the real taxis. So, by the end of 2026, Uber is actually going to be banned from the city center of Barcelona. What you should probably do is before you get on your flight, download the app called Free Now. It kind of works like Uber, but it will connect you to the real official taxi fleet of Barcelona. Or even better, just take the metro. This is the fastest and the cheapest way to get around Barcelona. But I do recommend that you avoid buying all individual tickets that cost you €2.90 per ticket. Whereas if you get this, which is called the T-casual, that has 10 rides, and then it's only €1.30 per ride. It works on the metro, the bus, and the train. All right, so so far we've been focusing on planning mistakes, but now we're going to cover some cultural mistakes, and we're going to start off with the best part, restaurants. So, let's head inside this tapas bar. Don't ignore how tapas work. I've been going to tapas restaurants ever since I was a little kid. I've been to hundreds of restaurants. So, how many should you have if you're two people? A lot of tourists kind of get confused by the concept and end up ordering a completely wrong way. So, tapas are small plates of different foods. You can have them as an appetizer or even as a full meal. If you want, you can just have some drinks and order like one or two tapas to share as like a small little snack to go with. If you want to make a full meal out of it, usually if you're two people, like five dishesthat should be plenty. What is the biggest mistake that people make? I kind of think the biggest mistake people make is thinking they have to like order everything all at once. They make like a big order. I think for me, the best way to do is just like order a couple of dishes, get started with it, then see if you want to add more or maybe go to a different place, have some more tapas dinner. You can kind of organize it the way you want. Don't expect too much from the service. A lot of travelers to Spain, especially Americans, will tell you that Spanish waiters, aren't really very polite, even a little bit rude. But that is the wrong way of looking at it. When it gets busy at a tapas restaurant, these people are hustling, bringing out all these small plates to the tables almost individually as they're prepared by the kitchen. They often are in charge of half a room by themselves. So, it's true that they're not going to come up to you and ask you how everything's tasting and check in on you every five minutes, but if you need something and you raise your hand, they're going to be on it in a second. So, don't mistake efficiency for rudeness. Don't go to a restaurant at 6:00 p.m. Spaniards eat late. A lot of the locals have lunch between 1 and 3:00 p.m. Dinner is often from 8 to 9:30. So, if you want to go to a nice small family-run restaurant and you show up there really early, you're either going to be the only one there or it's not even going to be open yet. This is not going to be true for every restaurant, but for a lot of them, don't trust La Boqueria Market. Don't get me wrong, La Boqueria is worth seeing, especially because you can see so many different kinds of Spanish food, but you do kind of have to be careful if you decide to eat here. Look at these churros. Churros are supposed to be nice and hot and crispy, freshly prepared, but the ones sitting in this counter are probably going to be here for a couple hours. So, they're going to be cold and soggy by the time you eat them. A lot of these stalls only survive because the foot traffic in La Boqueria is just so immense. One of the things you should try here is a Spanish ham. You can find this everywhere. It's one of the local specialties and it's super delicious. Or you can go to El Quim which has thousands of freebies. We went there in our last video and we had the squid with the fried eggs and we had some cheese croquettes and everything was just fantastic. It was so good I had to fight Kim for the last bite, literally with knife and fork. If there's one dish you need to be picky about, it's paella. So don't settle for bad paella. Locals will tell you there's a lot of mediocre ones here in Barcelona. So you have to go to a restaurant that really specializes in it. We went to Xiringuito Escriba. Hope I'm pronouncing that right. They have an open kitchen. You can watch them prepare it in these huge pans. And the seafood paella was amazing. Highly recommend it. And to help you find even more great food, we've put together a map with 20 great restaurants where we've been ourselves. Highly recommend it. I'll put in the description box below. Go and grab it. It's completely free. Now, we've come to cultural mistakes, which is very important because when you're in Barcelona, you probably want to blend in and you definitely don't want to do something that might offend the locals. So, don't dress inappropriately. Barcelona has a bit of a complicated relationship with tourism. There have been protests over the last few years and some locals have started pushing back against it quite openly. Now, that does not mean that people are hostile towards visitors because everyone we've met here has been super welcoming, but it is very important to be respectful. And one of the things that irritates locals the most is when tourists wander around the city in their beach outfits. So, with a bikini or bear chest, a towel draped around your shoulders. This makes people feel as though you think of their city as a resort. So, make sure to put on some proper clothes when you come back from the beach. And pay extra care when going into a church because Spain is a deeply religious country. And that means you cannot have any bare shoulders, bare knees, or cleavage showing if you are going into a church. Whether you're a guy or a girl, it does not matter. Don't assume the locals speak English. The good news about traveling around in Barcelona is that the tourist infrastructure is really good. If you go to buy tickets for the metro, the machine will be in English. If you're at one of the gaudy highlights, all the signs are going to be in English. But if you walk into a shop or restaurant in Barcelona and you start speaking in English and assume everyone will just follow your lead and understand, that is actually kind of rude because a lot of locals don't speak it. And you are kind of in their city. It makes a massive difference to your waiter, to your hotel receptionist if you just leave with 'buenos dias' or 'bon dia', which is Catalan. Which brings me to my next point. Don't forget you are in Catalonia because Barcelona is not just a Spanish city. It's also the capital of a Spanish region called Catalonia. And they have their own culture, their own language, and a very fierce pride in being different from the rest of Spain. So if you take the bus here, there's going to be signs in three different languages. In English (to help you out), in Spanish and in Catalan. If you go into a shop and you buy something, people don't say 'gracias' in Spanish, they say 'merci' in Catalan. And if you reply with 'merci' as well, you can just see their faces light up because it makes them feel so appreciated. So don't forget about this. Don't spend too much time in the Gothic Quarter. Of course, you have the beautiful cathedral right behind me. And then here you have the medieval alleyways. But the locals will tell you this isn't the Barcelona they live in because nowadays it has become very touristy. It's lined wall to wall with souvenir shops. Now I'm not saying to skip it but when you come to the beautiful famous Pont del Bisbe. Definitely take some pictures here. But it gets pretty crowded here. So just head two streets that way where you'll find the quiet Plaza San Felipe Nere where you have a beautiful church and a bar and you have it all to yourself. And second, leave some time to just walk some other areas too. One of my favorite things to do is just stroll the Passeig de Gracia. It has these grand buildings and on one side you find find this fairy tale towers and then you turn a corner and then you find tiny pedestrian alleyways that feel like secret. Don't have sangria with your dinner. If you're at a restaurant and your waiter is suggesting a big jug of sangria to go with your paella, that's a sign you're probably at a bit of a touristy spot because Spaniards don't typically do that. In Spain, sangria is more of a party drink. It doesn't pair well with a lot of foods because it's very sweet. So there are no fines for this particular behavior. But if you want to blend in a little bit more instead of sangria, go for a beer or wine with your dinner. Don't wait for the bill. Unlike in some other countries here in Spain, they won't bring you the bill automatically. You have to ask for it. In Spanish, it's 'la cuenta por favor'. It's not because they're being rude. It's just because they want to give you time to relax and just enjoy your meal. Don't just stay in the city. One of the coolest things about Barcelona is its location right on the coast of the Mediterranean Sea. And one of our favorite things we did here, was a sunset sailing trip. It was 2 hours out on the water with unlimited cava, tapas in a small group. It was amazing, especially after a full day of walking around the city. And if you want to do a full day trip, you can also go to Montserrat. It's an hour outside the city. It's a thousand year old monastery high up in the mountains around Barcelona. Incredible. We'll link those activities in the description box below in case you want to check them out. With everything you've learned in this video, I'm sure Barcelona is going to be an amazing trip for you. If you want to see our 3-day itinerary for the city, you can watch that video here. Or if you want to know 10 restaurants that are really worth your time, go and watch that here. Adios.

videos.transcript: Barcelona Spain, a city of golden streets, Gothic whispers, and Gaudí’s magical creations, captured my heart in ways I never expected. In this Top 10 Things To Do in Barcelona Vlog, I’ll show you everything you need to know before visiting — where to go, what to eat, how to get around, and the best things to do in Barcelona that will truly blow your mind. By the end of this Barcelona Travel Guide Vlog, you’ll find the most important information, and I’ll be waiting for your likes and specific questions in the comments. Walking along La Ramblas is like stepping into the heart of Barcelona, where street performers, flower stalls, and bustling cafés create a nonstop energy that pulls you forward. I loved watching artists sketch portraits while sipping coffee at an outdoor table, surrounded by both locals and tourists. At the end of the boulevard, you’ll arrive at Plaça de Catalunya, the city’s central square where old Barcelona meets the modern shopping districts. It’s a perfect place to start your day since almost every bus, tram, or tour seems to pass through here. If you’re craving a quieter local vibe, head into the Gràcia neighborhood, where narrow streets open into lively plazas with children playing and locals chatting. This area felt more authentic and less touristy to me, making it one of the best spots to slow down and experience everyday Barcelona life. The Gothic Quarter is a maze of medieval streets where every corner hides a piece of history and culture. Standing before the Cathedral of Barcelona, I was in awe of its Gothic architecture, and climbing to the rooftop rewarded me with panoramic views. Nearby, the Basílica de Santa Maria del Pi offers a quieter, spiritual atmosphere with its famous rose window flooding the interior with colorful light. One evening, I stumbled into Plaça Reial, a square lined with palm trees, glowing lanterns, and lively cafés. This became one of my favorite nightlife spots in Barcelona, especially with local musicians performing late into the night. Exploring the Gothic Quarter felt like traveling back in time, and it’s a must-see area for both first-timers and returning visitors. No trip to Barcelona is complete without tasting its food culture, and Mercat de la Boqueria is the ultimate place to begin. I wandered through colorful stalls filled with fresh fruit juices, Iberian ham, seafood, and endless tapas choices. The atmosphere is loud, fun, and chaotic, but that’s exactly what makes it so authentic. For a different food experience, head to El Nacional, a beautifully designed food hall with separate sections for tapas, seafood, and wine bars. I really enjoyed trying classic Catalan dishes there while soaking in the elegant atmosphere. These two culinary stops are the perfect way to experience Barcelona through its flavors and local traditions. Barcelona’s cultural life is everywhere, and it’s best explored by diving into its museums and historic venues. At Casa Padellà, home of the MUHBA, I walked through Roman ruins and medieval streets hidden beneath the city. It was like peeling back layers of time and seeing how Barcelona grew over the centuries. The Picasso Museum is another highlight, where you can admire the artist’s early works and understand his deep connection to the city. I also loved visiting El Born Center for Culture and Memory, where an old market hall now houses fascinating archaeological remains. Together, these places give you a deeper appreciation of Barcelona beyond its famous architecture. When I needed a break from the busy city, Ciutadella Park was the perfect escape with its giant fountain, boating lake, and peaceful lawns. Families, couples, and students all gather here, giving the park a warm and welcoming vibe. If you’re traveling with kids, the zoo inside the park can be a fun stop as well. Of course, Barcelona’s Barceloneta Beach is the city’s most popular outdoor hangout. I loved walking the boardwalk, watching people play volleyball, and even trying some beachside tapas. It’s the best spot to soak up the Mediterranean sun without leaving the city center. Barcelona’s waterfront has a charm of its own, starting with the Rambla de Mar, a scenic wooden walkway over the harbor. The views of yachts, the sea breeze, and the lively atmosphere make it perfect for an afternoon stroll. You’ll arrive at Maremagnum, a waterfront mall filled with shops, restaurants, and even an aquarium. It’s a great place to relax, shop, or grab dinner while watching the sun set over the water. Right nearby stands the Columbus Monument, a towering landmark you can actually climb for panoramic harbor views. This whole area gives a refreshing change from the crowded streets while still keeping you close to the action. Barcelona is synonymous with Gaudí, and his works are spread all across the city. At Casa Batlló, I was amazed by its colorful mosaics, dragon-like roof, and whimsical design. Nearby, Casa Milà, also known as La Pedrera, stood out with its wavy stone façade and surreal rooftop chimneys. But nothing compares to the Sagrada Família, Gaudí’s unfinished masterpiece and Barcelona’s most iconic landmark. Walking inside felt like stepping into a forest of stone pillars, with stained glass filling the space with vibrant colors. These architectural gems are must-see highlights that truly define the city’s artistic spirit. If you love unique architecture, Casa de les Punxes is a fairytale-like mansion that looks straight out of a storybook. Its pointed towers and modernist details make it one of the most photogenic buildings in Barcelona. For something more playful, Park Güell is filled with colorful mosaics, winding paths, and Gaudí’s signature creativity. I especially enjoyed sitting on the mosaic benches while taking in sweeping city views. For the very best panorama, head up to the Bunkers El Carmel, a hilltop viewpoint with a 360-degree view of the entire city. Watching the sunset from here was one of my most memorable moments in Barcelona. Montjuïc Hill is a destination in itself, filled with attractions and great views. Starting at Plaça d’Espanya, I admired the grand fountains and wide avenue leading up to the hill. The Arenas de Barcelona, once a bullring, has now been transformed into a modern shopping and dining hub with a rooftop terrace. Wandering through Poble Espanyol, an open-air museum, I experienced Spain’s diverse architecture and regional traditions in one place. The village-like setting with artisan shops and live performances made it feel both educational and entertaining. Montjuïc is one of the best spots to combine sightseeing, culture, and relaxation all in one area. As the sun sets, Montjuïc becomes even more magical with its evening attractions. I started at the Museu Nacional d’Art de Catalunya, where the terrace offered a spectacular view over the city. From there, I walked down to the Magic Fountain of Montjuïc, where the nightly light and water show amazed everyone watching. The music, colors, and dancing fountains created a perfect ending to a day in Barcelona. If you still have energy, make your way up to Montjuïc Castle, a historic fortress overlooking the harbor. Seeing the city lights from up here was the ultimate way to say goodnight to Barcelona. That’s the end of my Barcelona Travel Guide Vlog with everything you need to know before visiting this unforgettable city. Don’t forget to like, comment, and share this video, and subscribe to my channel so you never miss more adventures from around the world. And if you need more information, feel free to ask me in the comments — I’ll be happy to help you plan your trip.
